document.source_title: Cheap Missiles, Not Drones, Will Win the Next Air War
document.summary: The article discusses the evolving dynamics of aerial warfare, particularly in the context of the Ukraine conflict and Iranian drone technology. It argues that traditional propeller-driven drones are becoming less effective against faster, turbojet-powered drones, which complicates air defense strategies. The author advocates for a shift towards developing cheap, autonomous missiles as a more viable solution for counter-drone tactics, emphasizing the need for innovation in missile technology to keep pace with emerging threats.
key_takeaways: Turbojet-powered drones are outpacing propeller-driven systems in speed and altitude.
Ukraine's reliance on propeller-based interceptors is proving ineffective against modern threats.
Iran's 358 missile offers a more flexible and cost-effective counter-drone solution.
NATO's focus on propeller drones may hinder its ability to adapt to new aerial threats.
The development of low-cost, autonomous missiles could change the economics of air defense.
Logistics and autonomy will be crucial in future warfare, necessitating a reevaluation of drone roles.
document.why_it_matters: The article highlights the urgent need for NATO and Western defense establishments to adapt their strategies and investments in aerial warfare technologies. As adversaries innovate and improve their capabilities, understanding the limitations of current systems and prioritizing the development of effective countermeasures is critical for maintaining strategic advantages in future conflicts.

claims.claim_text: Propeller-driven drones are becoming less effective against turbojet-powered drones.
claims.evidence_text: Ukraine’s propeller-based interceptor drones have become much less effective at intercepting their targets due to the speed and altitude advantages of turbojet drones.

claims.claim_text: Iran's 358 missile is a more effective counter-drone solution than Western approaches.
claims.evidence_text: The 358 missile can efficiently intercept a broad class of aerial threats, including Shahed-type systems, and is reportedly priced at around $90,000 per unit.

claims.claim_text: NATO's investment in propeller drones may be misguided.
claims.evidence_text: NATO countries are betting too heavily on a class of systems that may already be on the wrong side of the curve.

entities.name: Shahed drones
entities.description: Iranian drones that have been modified to fly faster and at higher altitudes, complicating air defense.
entities.evidence_text: Russia is transforming slow, propeller-driven Shahed drones into cheap, missile-like systems by equipping them with turbojet engines.

entities.name: 358 missile
entities.description: An Iranian missile designed to intercept various aerial threats, including advanced drones.
entities.evidence_text: The 358 missile can efficiently intercept a broad class of aerial threats, including Shahed-type systems.

metrics.name: Speed of turbojet drones
metrics.evidence_text: Turbojet drones now fly four to five times faster (about 460 miles per hour, compared to 90 miles per hour).

metrics.name: Altitude of turbojet drones
metrics.evidence_text: Turbojet drones fly at higher altitudes (about 29,000 feet, versus 6,500 feet).

metrics.name: Cost of 358 missile
metrics.evidence_text: The 358 missile is reportedly priced at around $90,000 per unit.

metrics.name: Cost range of turbojet drones
metrics.evidence_text: If an adversary can potentially scale production of turbojet-powered drones to thousands of units at $20,000–$50,000 each.

citations.title: Cheap Missiles, Not Drones, Will Win the Next Air War

source_credibility.reason: The article presents a well-reasoned analysis of current military technology trends and their implications for future warfare.
